moat_name: Pet Vaccine Schedule by Breed & Age
source: AVMA, AAHA 2022 Canine Vaccination Guidelines, AKC
disclaimer: This schedule is for informational purposes only. Consult your veterinarian before vaccinating your dog—vaccination decisions depend on individual health, lifestyle, and local disease prevalence.

puppy_core_schedule.notes: First dose; primes immune system. Schedule 2-4 weeks apart from subsequent doses.
Second dose; accounts for declining maternal antibodies.
Final core series. Rabies provides lifelong protection; some states allow 3-year or annual formulations.
1-year or 3-year formulation available per state law.

adult_booster_schedule.notes: Research confirms ≥3 years protection; annual boosters no longer recommended.
Depends on formulation (1-year vs. 3-year) and state law.

requirements: DHPP booster every 3 years (or per vet discretion)
Rabies booster per state law (1-3 years)
Consider antibody titer testing instead of automatic boosters
senior_protocol.special_notes: Senior dogs may have adverse reactions to vaccines; delay non-core vaccines if illness present. Rabies medical exemptions available in some states for high-risk seniors.

non_core_vaccines.indication: Dogs in boarding, daycare, training, or dog parks
non_core_vaccines.initial_schedule: Single intranasal dose OR two injectable doses 2-4 weeks apart
non_core_vaccines.booster_frequency: Annual (or before boarding; may be as short as 6 months)
non_core_vaccines.notes: Intranasal route preferred for protection and ease of administration.

non_core_vaccines.indication: Endemic areas (Northeast, upper Midwest, Pacific Coast) with lifestyle tick exposure
non_core_vaccines.initial_schedule: Two doses 2-4 weeks apart, starting at 12+ weeks
non_core_vaccines.breed_risk: Dachshunds: 49.4 adverse events/10,000 visits (higher than average); Labs, Goldens, GSDs: standard risk
non_core_vaccines.breed_mitigation: Dachshunds in endemic areas—consider benefit/risk; stagger vaccines 2 weeks apart to reduce reactions
non_core_vaccines.geographic_expansion: Now endemic in Pacific Coast (WA, OR, CA); expanding southward yearly
non_core_vaccines.clinical_note: Vaccine reduces infection severity but does not fully prevent infection with high bacterial loads

non_core_vaccines.indication: All dogs considered at-risk; water exposure, wildlife carriers (raccoons, skunks)
non_core_vaccines.initial_schedule: Two doses at 8 and 12 weeks (puppies), OR starting at 12+ weeks for older dogs; 2-4 weeks apart
non_core_vaccines.breed_risk: No breed predisposition; all dogs in North America at risk
non_core_vaccines.clinical_note: Only serovar-matched protection; cross-protection is limited. Yearly boosters required.

non_core_vaccines.indication: Dogs in boarding, shelters, high-density environments, or training
non_core_vaccines.notes: Emerging concern in shelters and boarding facilities.

mitigation_strategies: Stagger vaccines 2+ weeks apart (separate core and non-core)
Consider single-antigen vaccines vs. combination products
Discuss non-core vaccine risk/benefit for small breeds

large_giant_breeds.adverse_event_rate: Inversely proportional to body weight; lower than small breeds
special_considerations: Rabies response: slightly lower antibody titers possible; recommend 2-dose initial series
State rabies laws may require annual boosters despite research

geographic_vaccine_recommendations.notes: Lyme is primary concern; two-dose initial series recommended for puppies in endemic counties

geographic_vaccine_recommendations.region: Pacific Coast (WA, OR, CA—Lyme expansion zone)
geographic_vaccine_recommendations.notes: Lyme now endemic; expansion ongoing. Tick exposure high in forested areas.
Lyme not endemic but expanding; Leptospirosis risk from wildlife and water exposure
High-density environments increase respiratory disease risk

vaccine_adverse_events.definition: Mild (fever, soreness) vs. Severe (anaphylaxis, collapse, autoimmune disease)
vaccine_adverse_events.reporting: Report to veterinarian and consider reporting to VAERS (Veterinary Adverse Event Reporting System)

titer_testing_alternative.rabies_limitation: Titer testing NOT validated for rabies; legal requirements supersede test results in all states
titer_testing_alternative.cost_trend: Declining; increasingly accessible for dogs with prior adverse reactions
use_cases: Senior dogs with adverse event history
